source.title: The neural development of empathy is sensitive to caregiving and early trauma

confidence_rationale: This is the most directly relevant paper for INV-043 in this set. Levy et al. followed 84 children across the first decade of life, measuring parenting quality, child temperament, and anxiety disorders, then assessed empathy-related neural responses (MEG) at preadolescence. The key finding: mother-child synchrony across childhood predicts the neural architecture of empathy in preadolescents. Children who experienced early trauma (abuse/neglect) showed impaired empathy-related neural responses. This is a longitudinal human study demonstrating that caregiver experience is necessary -- not merely helpful -- for normal empathy development, and that this necessity has a neural substrate. The paper supports INV-043 directly because it shows the architecture (empathy-capable neural circuits) is present but that its motivational/functional activation requires a history of attuned caregiving. Confidence 0.68 because this is human longitudinal neuroscience (high source quality, moderate transfer risk to AI), and the empathy finding directly concerns the social-emotional substrate INV-043 invokes.
failure_signatures: If empathy-related neural responses at preadolescence showed no relationship to early caregiving quality -- if children with neglectful or abusive caregiving showed equivalent empathy neural responses -- the developmental necessity claim would be falsified.

If early trauma's effect on empathy-related neural responses were fully mediated by anxiety disorders or temperament rather than caregiving per se, the caregiver-specific necessity claim would be weakened.

If INV-043 were falsified: if REE systems trained without any caregiving-analog interaction history showed equivalent ethical activation to those with such history, the developmental necessity claim would not hold.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Eighty-four children were followed from early childhood to preadolescence. Parenting quality (mother-child synchrony) across childhood predicted the neural profile of empathy at preadolescence as measured by MEG responses to observed distress. Children who experienced early trauma showed reduced empathy-related neural responses. The neural architecture of empathy (assessed as distributed responses spanning affective and cognitive empathy structures) develops in a manner that is sensitive to both caregiving quality and adversity history -- it is not fixed by genetic endowment alone.
mapping.ree_translation: INV-043 claims that REE's ethical capacity, though architecturally present, requires caregiving experience to become motivationally active. Levy et al. provide the strongest human longitudinal evidence that the analogous biological claim holds: empathy-relevant neural circuits exist in all children, but their functional organisation at preadolescence is shaped by the quality of early caregiver experience. Mother-child synchrony -- the experience of being attended to, responded to, and held in mind -- predicts which children's empathy circuitry develops into a broad, functionally integrated response versus a reduced one. In REE terms: the z_goal seeding process, the harm-salience weighting, and the prosocial action selection that constitute ethical agency may all depend on an analogous 'synchrony history' in the training or developmental process.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The central transfer risk is from human developmental neuroscience to AI architecture. Levy et al. describe biological neural development across a decade of lived experience with real caregivers. The mechanisms -- mother-child synchrony, HPA axis calibration, prefrontal-limbic connectivity maturation -- do not have direct computational analogs in REE. INV-043's claim that an AI system requires 'caregiving experience' to activate ethical capacity is either a metaphor (interaction history with ethical training signal) or a substantive architectural hypothesis (some training regime analog to developmental synchrony is required). This paper supports the metaphorical reading strongly, but the substantive architectural claim would require entirely different evidence. Additionally, the paper is a longitudinal observational study, not an experimental manipulation -- it establishes correlation between caregiving and neural empathy development, not strict causal necessity.
mapping.source_context: Human longitudinal study; n=84 children followed from birth/early childhood to preadolescence (~10 years); Israeli community sample; MEG at preadolescence while observing others' distress; parenting measured via observational coding of mother-child synchrony; anxiety disorder diagnoses via structured interview. Feldman lab, Bar-Ilan/IDC Herzliya.

confidence_components.notes: Nature Communications 2019, peer-reviewed, longitudinal human neuroscience with objective neural measures (MEG) and observational coding of caregiving. Source quality very high for developmental neuroscience. Mapping fidelity is the best in this set because the paper directly concerns the developmental activation of empathy-relevant circuits by caregiving experience, which is the core claim of INV-043. Transfer risk is the dominant limitation: human biological neural development to AI architectural activation is a large conceptual step. Transfer_risk 0.35 reflects the strength of the structural analogy (caregiving activates latent empathy capacity) balanced against the lack of any mechanistic pathway from biological caregiving to AI training dynamics.
